content: If t is a parameter, then $$x = a\left( {t + {1 \over t}} \right)$$, $$y = b\left( {t - {1 \over t}} \right)$$ represents
explanation:
$$x = a\left( {t + {1 \over t}} \right)$$, $$y = b\left( {t - {1 \over t}} \right)$$

or, $$t + {1 \over t} = {x \over a}$$ ..... (1)

$$t - {1 \over t} = {y \over b}$$ ....... (2)

Since $${\left( {t + {1 \over t}} \right)^2} - {\left( {t - {1 \over t}} \right)^2} = 4$$ (using 1 and 2)

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 4 \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over {4{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {4{b^2}}} = 1$$

This is equation of hyperbola.

content: If the line ax + by + c = 0 is a tangent to the curve xy = 4, then
explanation:
$$\because$$ xy = 4

$$\therefore$$ $$1y + x{{dy} \over {dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = - {y \over x}$$

$$\therefore$$ Any point on xy = 4 may be taken as $$P\left( {2t,{2 \over t}} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ $${\left[ {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right]_{at\,P}} = - {{2/t} \over {2t}} = - {1 \over {{t^2}}}$$ = Slope of tangent to xy = 4 at p

If $$ax + by + c = 0$$ be tangent to xy = 4 at P, then

$$ - {1 \over {{t^2}}} = - {a \over b}$$ (Slope of ax + by + c = 0)

$${t^2} = {b \over a}$$ $$\because$$ $${t^2} > 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $${b \over a} > 0$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ Either a < 0, b < 0 or a > 0, b > 0

content: For different values of $$\alpha$$, the locus of the point of intersection of the two straight lines $$\sqrt 3 x - y - 4\sqrt 3 \alpha = 0$$ and $$\sqrt 3 \alpha x + \alpha y - 4\sqrt 3 = 0$$ is
explanation:
Two lines are : $$\sqrt 3 x - y = 4\sqrt 3 \alpha $$ ...... (1)

& $$(\sqrt 3 x + y)\alpha = 4\sqrt 3 $$ ...... (2)

On multiplying, $$3{x^2} - {y^2} = 48$$

$${{{x^2}} \over {16}} - {{{y^2}} \over {48}} = 1$$ (Hyperbola)

$$\therefore$$ $$e = \sqrt {1 + {{48} \over {16}}} = 2$$

content: The eccentricity of the hyperbola $$4{x^2} - 9{y^2} = 36$$ is

In a plane $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$ are the position vectors of two points A and B respectively. A point $P$ with position vector $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$$ moves on that plane in such a way that $$|\overrightarrow{\vec{r}}-\vec{a}| \sim|\vec{r}-\vec{b}|=c$$ (real constant). The locus of P is a conic section whose eccentricity is
The locus of the midpoint of the system of parallel chords parallel to the line $$y=2 x$$ to the hyperbola $$9 x^2-4 y^2=36$$ is
If a hyperbola passes through the point P($$\sqrt2$$, $$\sqrt3$$) and has foci at ($$\pm$$ 2, 0), then the tangent to this hyperbola at P is
explanation:
Focii of hyperbola $$( \pm a e, 0)=( \pm 2,0)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad a e=2$$ ..... (i)

Let equation of hyperbola be $$\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$$

It passes through $$P(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3})$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^2}-\frac{3}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^2}-\frac{3}{a^2\left(e^2-1\right)}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{a^2}-\frac{3}{a^2 e^2-a^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^2}-\frac{3}{4-a^2}=1 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$a^2=x$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{x}-\frac{3}{4-x}=1$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad 8-2 x-3 x=4 x-x^2$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad x^2-9 x+8=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)(x-8)=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad x=1,8$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad {a}^2=1,8$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad b^2=4-a^2=3,-4$$

But, $$b^2 \neq-4$$

$$\therefore \quad a^2=1, b^2=3$$

So, equation of hyperbola is $$\frac{x^2}{1}-\frac{y^2}{3}=1$$

Equation of tangent at $$P(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3})$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x \cdot \sqrt{2}}{1}-\frac{y \cdot \sqrt{3}}{3}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=x \sqrt{6}-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The average length of all vertical chords of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1,a \le x \le 2a$$, is :
explanation:
The equation of the hyperbola is given by $\frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$.

For a given value of $x$ in the interval $[a, 2a]$, the corresponding $y$ values can be calculated from the hyperbola equation as follows :

$\frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$

Solving for $y$, we get $y = \pm b\sqrt{\frac{x^2}{a^2} - 1}$.

The length of a vertical chord at a given $x$ value will be the difference of the $y$ values, which gives us :

Length of chord = $2b\sqrt{\frac{x^2}{a^2} - 1}$

To calculate the average length of all vertical chords from $x = a$ to $x = 2a$, we take the definite integral of this function over that interval, and divide by the length of the interval :

Average length of chord = $\frac{1}{2a - a} \int\limits_{a}^{2a} 2b \sqrt{\frac{x^2}{a^2} - 1} dx$

Simplifying this gives us :

Average length of chord = $\frac{2b}{a} \int\limits_{a}^{2a} \sqrt{\frac{x^2}{a^2} - 1} dx$

$\therefore$ Average length of vertical chord from $a$ to $2 a$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{2 \int\limits_a^{2 a} y d x}{\int\limits_a^2 d x}=\frac{2 \int\limits_a^{2 a} \frac{b}{a} \sqrt{x^2-a^2} d x}{(x)_a^{2 a}} \\\\ & =\frac{\frac{2 b}{a} \int\limits_a^{2 a} \sqrt{x^2-a^2} d x}{a}=\frac{2 b}{a^2} \int\limits_0^{2 a} \sqrt{x^2-a^2} d x \\\\ & =\frac{2 b}{a^2}\left(\frac{x}{3} \sqrt{x^2-a^2}-\frac{a^2}{2} \ln \left|x+\sqrt{x^2-a^2}\right|\right)_a^{2 a} \\\\ & =\frac{2 b}{a^2}\left[\frac{(2 a) \sqrt{4 a^2-a^2}}{2}-\frac{a^2 \ln \left|2 a+\sqrt{4 a^2-a^2}\right|}{2}\right. \\\\ & \left.-\frac{a \sqrt{a^2-a^2}}{2}+\frac{a^2 \ln \left|a+\sqrt{a^2-a^2}\right|}{2}\right] \\\\ & =\frac{2 b}{a^2}\left[\sqrt{3} a^2-\frac{\left.a^2 \ln \mid 2+\sqrt{3}\right) a \mid}{2}+\frac{a^2 \ln |a|}{2}\right] \\\\ & =2 b\left(\sqrt{3}+\frac{\ln \left|\frac{a}{(2+\sqrt{3}) a}\right|}{2}\right) \\\\ & =b(2 \sqrt{3}-\ln |2+\sqrt{3}|) \end{aligned} $$

content: Let $$A(2\sec \theta ,3\tan \theta )$$ and $$B(2\sec \phi ,3\tan \phi )$$ where $$\theta + \phi = {\pi \over 2}$$ be two points on the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over 4} - {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$. If ($$\alpha,\beta$$) is the point of intersection of normals to the hyperbola at A and B, then $$\beta$$ is equal to
explanation:
We have, hyperbola $$\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{9}=1$$

$$A(2 \sec \theta, 3 \tan \theta), B(2 \sec \phi, 3 \tan \phi)$$

Equation of normal in parametric form gives equation of normal at $$A$$ and $$B$$ as:

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x \cos \theta+3 y \cot \theta=13 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & 2 x \cos \phi+3 y \cot \phi=13 \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { Also, } \theta+\phi=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \phi=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Eq. (ii) becomes,

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)+3 y \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=13 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x \sin \theta+3 y \tan \theta=13 \quad \text{.... (iii)}\\ & \sin \theta \times(1)-\cos \theta \times(3), \\ & 3 y(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)=13(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{-13}{3} \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{-13}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$P(3\sec \theta ,2\tan \theta )$$ and $$Q(3\sec \phi ,2\tan \phi )$$ be two points on $${{{x^2}} \over 9} - {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$ such that $$\theta + \phi = {\pi \over 2},0 < \theta ,\phi < {\pi \over 2}$$. Then the ordinate of the point of intersection of the normals at P and Q is
explanation:
An equation of the normal at P is

$$3x\cos \theta + 2y\cot \theta = 9 + 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x + xy\cos ec\theta = 13\sec \theta $$ ..... (i)

An equation of normal at Q is

$$3x + 2y\cos ec\phi = 13\sec \phi $$ ..... (ii)

On subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get

$$2y[\cos ec\theta - \cos ec\phi ] = 13(\sec \theta - \sec \phi )]$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2y[\cos ec\theta - \sec \theta ] = 13(\sec \theta - \cos ec\phi )]$$ [$$\because$$ $$\phi = {\pi \over 2} - 0$$]

$$y = - {{13} \over 2}$$

content: PQ is a double ordinate of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ such that $$\Delta OPQ$$ is an equilateral triangle, O being the centre of the hyperbola. Then the eccentricity e of the hyperbola satisfies
explanation:
Let the coordinates of P be $$(h,k)$$.

$$\therefore$$ $$PQ=2k$$ and $$OP=OQ = PQ$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt {{h^2} + {k^2}} = 2k \Rightarrow {h^2} + {k^2} = 4{k^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {h^2} = 3{k^2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$(h,k)$$ lies on hyperbola $$ \Rightarrow {{{h^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{k^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{3{k^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{k^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{3{b^2} - {a^2}} \over {{a^2}{b^2}}} = {1 \over {{k^2}}} > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3 \over {{a^2}}} - {1 \over {{b^2}}} > 0 \Rightarrow {3 \over {{a^2}}} > {1 \over {{b^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} > {1 \over 3} \Rightarrow {e^2} - 1 > {1 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^2} > {4 \over 3} \Rightarrow e > 2/\sqrt 3 $$

content: The normal to a curve at P(x, y) meets the X-axis at G. If the distance of G from the origin is twice the abscissa of P then the curve is
explanation:
We know, slope of tangent = $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$

and slope of normal = $$ - {{dx} \over {dy}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of normal,

$$Y - y = - {{dx} \over {dy}}(X - x)$$

At point G, y = 0

$$\therefore$$ $$0 - y = - {{dx} \over {dy}}(X - x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x = X$$

$$\therefore$$ $$G = \left( {y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x,0} \right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Distance of G from origin

$$OG = y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x$$

Given, OG = 2 $$\times$$ abscissa of p

= 2 $$\times$$ x-coordinate of p

= 2 x

$$\therefore$$ $$y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x = 2x$$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {y\,dy = \int {x\,dx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{y^2}} \over 2} = {{{x^2}} \over 2} + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} - {x^2} = 2c$$

Which is rectangular hyperbola.

content: The locus of the centre of a variable circle which always touches two given circles externally is
explanation:
According to the question, we have


CC1 = r + r1 and

CC2 = r + r2

$$\therefore$$ CC2 $$-$$ CC1 = (r + r2) $$-$$ (r + r1) = r2 $$-$$ r1

$$\Rightarrow$$ Locus of centre is Hyperbola.

content: A double ordinate PQ of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ is such that $$\Delta OPQ$$ is equilateral, O being the centre of the hyperbola. Then the eccentricity e satisfies the relation
explanation:
A double PQ of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

Such that $$\Delta OPQ$$ is an equilateral where O is origin.

Let P(a sec $$\theta $$, b tan $$\theta $$) and Q(a sec $$\theta $$, $$ - $$ b tan $$\theta $$)

$$\Delta OPQ$$ is an eaquilateral

$$ \therefore $$ OP = PQ = OQ

$${a^2}{\sec ^2}\theta + {b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta = 4{b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta $$

$${a^2}{\sec ^2}\theta = 3{b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta $$

$${a^2}{\sec ^2}\theta = 3{b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta $$

$${{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {{{{\sec }^2}\theta } \over {3{{\tan }^2}\theta }} = {1 \over 3}\cos e{c^2}\theta $$

$$e = \sqrt {1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = } \sqrt {1 + {{\cos e{c^2}\theta } \over 3}} = {1 \over {\sqrt 3 }}\sqrt {3 + \cos e{c^2}\theta } $$

$$e > {2 \over {\sqrt 3 }}$$ [$$ \because $$ cosec2 $$\theta $$ $$ \ge $$ 1]

content: Let P(4, 3) be a point on the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$. If the normal at P intersects the X-axis at (16, 0), then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
explanation:
Given hyperbola equation

$$H:{{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

P (4, 3) lie on H

$$ \Rightarrow {{16} \over {{a^2}}} - {9 \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

Normal equation at P (4, 3) for H

$${a^2}{y_1}(x - {x_1}) + {b^2}{x_1}(y - {y_1}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{a^2}(x - 4) + 4{b^2}(y - 3) = 0$$

Normal cuts the X-axis at (16, 0)

$$ \Rightarrow 3{a^2}(16 - 4) + 4{b^2}(0 - 3) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{a^2}(12) + 4{b^2}( - 3) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 36{a^2} - 12{b^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{a^2} - {b^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{a^2} = {b^2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Eccentricity of the hyperbola is

$$e = {{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} } \over a}$$

$$ = {{\sqrt {{a^2} + 3{a^2}} } \over a} = {{\sqrt {4{a^2}} } \over a} = {{2a} \over a} = 2$$

content: For the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{{\cos }^2}\alpha }} - {{{y^2}} \over {{{\sin }^2}\alpha }} = 1$$, which of the following remains fixed when $$\alpha$$ varies?
explanation:
Given, equation of hyperbola is

$${{{x^2}} \over {{{\cos }^2}\alpha }} - {{{y^2}} \over {{{\sin }^2}\alpha }} = 1$$

Here, $${a^2} = {\cos ^2}\alpha $$ and $${b^2} = {\sin ^2}\alpha $$

[i.e. comparing with standard equation $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$]

We know that, foci = $$( \pm ae,0)$$,

where, $$ae = \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} = \sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\sin }^2}\alpha } = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ foci $$( \pm 1,0)$$, where vertices are ($$ \pm $$ cos $$\alpha$$, 0)

Eccentricity, ae = 1 or $$e = {1 \over {\cos \alpha }}$$.

Hence, foci remains constant with change in $$\alpha$$.

content: The equation of the directrices of the hyperbola $$3{x^2} - 3{y^2} - 18x + 12y + 2 = 0$$ is
explanation:
Given equation is

$$3{x^2} - 3{y^2} - 18x + 12y + 2 = 0$$

It can be written as

$${{{{(x - 3)}^2}} \over {{{\left( {\sqrt {{{13} \over 3}} } \right)}^2}}} - {{{{(y - 2)}^2}} \over {{{\left( {\sqrt {{{13} \over 3}} } \right)}^2}}} = 1$$

here, $$a = b = \sqrt {{{13} \over 3}} $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$e = \sqrt {1 + {b \over {{a^2}}}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {1 + 1} $$ [$$ \because $$ a = b]

$$ = \sqrt 2 $$

$$ \therefore $$ Equation of the directrix are

$$x = 3 \pm \sqrt {{{13} \over 6}} $$

content: The length of conjugate axis of a hyperbola is greater than the length of transverse axis. Then, the eccentricity e is
explanation:
Let the length of conjugate axis = b

and the length of transvers axis = a

Given that, b > a

Now, b2 > a2

$$ \therefore $$ Eccentricity (e) = $$\sqrt {1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}} $$

$${e^2} = {\left( {\sqrt {1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}} } \right)^2} > 2$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$e > \sqrt 2 $$

content: A point is in motion along a hyperbola $$y = {{10} \over x}$$ so that its abscissa x increases uniformly at a rate of 1 unit per second. Then, the rate of change of its ordinate when the point passes through (5, 2)

options.content: increases at the rate of $${1 \over 2}$$ unit per second
decreases at the rate of $${1 \over 2}$$ unit per second
decreases at the rate of $${2 \over 5}$$ unit per second
increases at the rate of $${2 \over 5}$$ unit per second

explanation:
We have,

$${{dx} \over {dt}} = 1$$ and $$y = {{10} \over x}$$

Now, $$y = {{10} \over x}$$

On differentiating both the sides w.r.t. t, we get

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dt}} = {{ - 10} \over {{x^2}}} \times {{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$ = {{ - 10} \over {{{(5)}^2}}} \times 1$$

$$ = {{ - 10} \over {25}} = {{ - 2} \over 5}$$ $$\left[ {x = 10,\,{{dx} \over {dt}} = 1} \right]$$

$$ \therefore $$ y decreases at the rate of $${2 \over 5}$$ unit per second.

content: Let the eccentricity of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ be reciprocal to that of the ellipse x<sup>2</sup> + 9y<sup>2</sup> = 9, then the ratio of a<sup>2</sup> : b<sup>2</sup> equals to
explanation:
Given equation of ellipses is x2 + 9y2 = 9

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 1} = 1$$

Here, a = 3, b = 1

$$c = \sqrt {{{(3)}^2} - {{(1)}^2}} = \sqrt 8 $$

$$ \therefore $$ Eccentricity of ellipse, $$e = {c \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = {{\sqrt 8 } \over 3}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Eccentricity of hyperbola $$a = {3 \over {\sqrt 8 }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {9 \over 8}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {1 \over 8}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2}:{b^2} = 8:1$$

content: Let P be the foot of the perpendicular from focus S of hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ on the line bx $$-$$ ay = 0 and let C be the centre of the hyperbola. Then, the area of the rectangle whose sides are equal to that of SP and CP is
explanation:
Given, equation of hyperbola is

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$


From figure,

$$SP = \left| {{{abe} \over {\sqrt {{b^2} + {a^2}} }}} \right|$$

$$ = \left| {{{abe} \over {ae}}} \right| = b$$

and CS = ae

Again, $$\Delta SPC$$ is right angled triangle at P.

$$ \therefore $$ $$CP = \sqrt {C{S^2} - S{P^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{a^2}{e^2} - {b^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{a^2}\left( {1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}} \right) - {b^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} - {b^2}} = a$$

$$ \therefore $$ Area of rectangle = $$CP \times SP = ab$$

content: The line segment joining the foci of the hyperbola $${x^2} - {y^2} + 1 = 0$$ is one of the diameters of a circle. The equation of the circle is
explanation:
Given, equation of hyperbola is

$${x^2} - {y^2} + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} - {x^2} = 1$$

On comparing it with $${{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} - {{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = 1$$, we get

a = b = 1

Now, $$e = \sqrt {1 + {{{a^2}} \over {{b^2}}}} = \sqrt {1 + {1 \over 1}} = \sqrt 2 $$

$$ \therefore $$ Foci = $$(0, \pm \,be) = (0,\, \pm \,\sqrt 2 )$$

Since, line joining foci of hyperbola is diameter of circle.

$$ \therefore $$ Centre of circle $$ = \left( {{{0 + 0} \over 2},{{\sqrt 2 - \sqrt 2 } \over 2}} \right) = (0,0)$$

and radius $$ = {1 \over 2}\sqrt {{{(0 - 0)}^2} + (\sqrt 2 - {{( - \sqrt {2)} )}^2}} $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\sqrt {{{(2\sqrt 2 )}^2}} = \sqrt 2 $$

$$ \therefore $$ Equation of circle will be

$${(x - 0)^2} + {(y - 0)^2} = {(\sqrt 2 )^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} = 2$$

content: Let A($$-$$ 1, 0) and B(2, 0) be two points. A point M moves in the plane in such a way that $$\angle MBA$$ = 2$$\angle MAB$$. Then, the point M moves along
explanation:
Let $$\angle MAB = \theta $$, then $$\angle MBA = 2\theta $$

$$\tan \theta = {k \over {1 + h}}$$ and $$\tan 2\theta {k \over {2 - h}}$$

then $$\tan \theta = {{2\tan \theta } \over {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }} = {k \over {2 - h}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2(k/1 + h)} \over {1 - {{(k/1 + h)}^2}}} = {k \over {2 - h}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2/(1 + h)} \over {{{{{(1 + h)}^2} - {k^2}} \over {{{(1 + h)}^2}}}}} = {1 \over {2 - h}}$$

$$\eqalign{ & \Rightarrow {2 \over {1 + h}} \times {{{{(1 + h)}^2}} \over {{{(1 + h)}^2} - {k^2}}} = {1 \over {2 - h}} \cr & \cr} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{2(1 + h)} \over {{{(1 + h)}^2} - {k^2}}} = {1 \over {2 - h}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {h^2} + 2h - {k^2} = 2(1 + h)(2 - h)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {h^2} + 2h - {k^2} = 2(2 - h + 2h - {h^2})$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {h^2} + 2h - {k^2} = 2(2 + h - {h^2})$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {h^2} + 2h - {k^2} = 4 + 2h - 2{h^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + 3{h^2} - {k^2} = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{h^2} - {k^2} = 3$$

which represents hyperbola (d).

content: If PQ is a double ordinate of the hyperbola $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ such that $$\Delta OPQ$$ is equilateral. O being the centre. Then, the eccentricity e satisfies
explanation:
Given equation of hyperbola is $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ and $$\Delta OPQ$$ is an equilateral triangle. PQ is double ordinate of the hyperbola.



Let the coordinates of P and Q be (a sec$$\theta$$, b tan$$\theta$$) and (a sec$$\theta$$ $$-$$ b tan$$\theta$$) respectively.

In $$\Delta$$OPQ,

$$OP = PQ$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2}{\sec ^2}\theta + {b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta = {(2b\tan \theta )^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2}{\sec ^2}\theta = 3{b^2}{\tan ^2}\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^2}\theta = {{{a^2}} \over {3{b^2}}}$$

Now, $${\sin ^2}\theta < 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{a^2}} \over {3{b^2}}} < 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} > {1 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 + {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} > {4 \over 3}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${e^2} > {4 \over 3} \Rightarrow e > {2 \over {\sqrt 3 }}$$

content: The line y = x + 5 touches
explanation:
Given $$y = x + 5$$

Comparing with $$y = mx + c$$

$$m = 1$$ and $$c = 5$$

Solve by option

Option (a) Condition of tangency

$$c = {a \over m}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5 = {5 \over 1}$$

which is true.

Option (b) $$9{x^2} + 16{y^2} = 144$$

$${{{x^2}} \over {16}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$

Condition of tangency $${c^2} = {a^2}{m^2} + {b^2}$$

$$25 = 16 \times 1 + 9 = 25$$

which is true.

Option (c) $${{{x^2}} \over {29}} - {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$

$$\because$$ Condition of tangency

$${c^2} = {a^2}{m^2} - {b^2}$$

$$25 = 29 \times 1 - 4 = 25$$

which is true.

Option (d) Now length of perpendicular from centre (0, 0) to the line $$y = x + 5$$ is $${{|5|} \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$.

i.e. $${5 \over {\sqrt 2 }} \ne $$ radius (5)

content: Equation of a tangent to the hyperbola 5x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ y<sup>2</sup> = 5 and which passes through an external point (2, 8) is
explanation:
Given equation of hyperbola can be write as

$${{{x^2}} \over 1} - {{{y^2}} \over {{{(\sqrt 5 )}^2}}} = 1$$

here, a = 1 and b = $${\sqrt 5 }$$

Since, $$y = mx \pm \sqrt {{a^2}{m^2} - {b^2}} $$

$$y = mx \pm \sqrt {{m^2} - 5} $$

$$ \Rightarrow {(8 - 2m)^2} = {(\sqrt {{m^2} - 5} )^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(8 - 2m)^2} = {m^2} - 5$$

$$ \Rightarrow 64 + 4{m^2} - 32m = {m^2} - 5$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3{m^2} - 32m + 69 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow m = {{22} \over 3},3$$ [by solving equation]

When $$m = {{22} \over 3}$$, then equation of tangent

$$(y - 8) = {{22} \over 3}(x - 2)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3y - 24 = 23x - 46$$

$$ \Rightarrow 23x - 3y - 46 + 24 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 23x - 3y - 22 = 0$$

When m = 3, then equation of tangent

$$(y - 8) = 3(x - 2)$$

$$ \Rightarrow y - 8 = 3x - 6$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x - y - 6 + 8 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x - y + 2 = 0$$

Hence, option (a) and (c) are correct.

content: A hyperbola, having the transverse axis of length 2sin$$\theta$$ is confocal wit6h the ellipse 3x<sup>2</sup> + 4y<sup>2</sup> = 12. Its equation is
explanation:
Given, $$2{a_1} = 2\sin \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {a_1} = \sin \theta $$

and $$3{x^2} + 4{y^2} = 12$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over 4} + {{{y^2}} \over 3} = 1$$

Here, $${a^2} = 4$$ and $${b^2} = 3$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3 = 4(1 + {e^2})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^2} = 1 - {3 \over 4} = {1 \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = {1 \over 2}$$

Focus, $$F(ae,0) = F\left( {2 \times {1 \over 2},0} \right)$$

$$ = F(1,0)$$

For hyperbola foci are same

$${a_1}{e_1} = ae = 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$(\sin \theta ){e_1} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e_1} = \cos ec\theta $$

and $$b_1^2 = a_1^2(e_1^2 - 1) = a_1^2e_1^2 - a_1^2$$

$$ \Rightarrow b_1^2 = 1 - {\sin ^2}\theta = {\cos ^2}\theta $$

$${{{x^2}} \over {a_1^2}} - {{{y^2}} \over {b_1^2}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over {{{\sin }^2}\theta }} - {{{y^2}} \over {{{\cos }^2}\theta }} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2}\cos e{c^2}\theta - {y^2}{\sec ^2}\theta = 1$$
